course_title: Essentials of Chinese Language in Social Life

learning_outcomes.description: Analyse the form, meaning, and function of languages with regard to their contexts in everyday life, including workplace interactions.
Develop a comprehensive understanding of the historical development of Chinese languages.
Understand its social and regional variances.
Become a skillful user of languages and an intercultural communicator.
Demonstrate respect for cultural and social diversity and for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and perspectives.
Demonstrate knowledge and respect for ethics and ethical standards in completing assessment tasks.
Lead self and others in group activities.
Be confident dealing with ambiguity and uncertainty.
Present persuasive oral and written arguments.
Be empathetic of other people, cultures, and knowledge systems.

assessments.due_date:

Week 7 – Week 9

In class presentation.

Each student will be assigned a timeslot to complete the presentation between September 9 to 25 September, 2026. Details will be published on Blackboard closer to date.

A student’s assigned timeslot will be considered the due date for this assessment.
assessments.task_description:
CHIN2600 Project 1: In-Class English Presentation

Purposes: Identify a real-world and language-centered problem. Use the theories or concepts learned in class to explain the problem. Communicate your preliminary findings to others in English, particularly to those unfamiliar with Chinese language and culture. Preparation for the final project.   

Due Date: Week 7 – Week 9 in class 

Weight: 60 %

Task Description:

Present to others in English how the theories/concepts you have learned from CHIN2600 can be used to explain a problem in your social life. You are highly encouraged to develop this project into your final project.  

Details: You will need to identify a research topic about real-world, language-based problems; explain why it is a problem by including all aspects; justify why the problems are critical by reviewing existing discussions; connect the problems to an aspect of Chinese language discussed in the class; explain which theory or concept taught in class can better explain the problems; collect some “social interactional” data to illustrate the problem (e.g., recordings of social interactions or online discussion); use concepts/theories taught in class to analyse an example from the data; and present your findings. Overall, you should make extensive research on your topic and show your understanding in the presentation with multiple sources. Make your presentation engaging and insightful. When collecting and analysing language data, always start with a social relationship or identity and then explore related language features, rather than the other way around.          

Format: The presentation is in-person and in-class and should last no longer than 6 minutes. Please use a PowerPoint presentation or any visual aids to present your project. If you are using PowerPoint, include only keywords or phrases on the slides, not sentences from your script. Avoid simply reading from slides during the presentation. You may use a palm card or note to help you deliver the presentation, but if you aim for higher marks, you should avoid using any of them to demonstrate that you can improvise without being constrained by the script. You will find that this helps you speak more naturally because you give more attention to the audience. Your time limit is 6 minutes, and a minimum of 5 minutes and 40 seconds.  Make sure you stay within this timeframe. The presentation will be followed by a 3-minute question-and-answer session with the instructor and potentially classmates.

Presentation schedule: To be announced on Blackboard during Week 4. 

Submission: Upload your PowerPoint slides to Blackboard (CHIN2600 Blackboard > Assessment > In-Class English Presentation > PowerPoint Slides Submission) the day before your presentation. 

What to include in your presentation:

  1. What is your topic? Why is it an important problem according to the existing discussions in news media, government policy, or AI search?    
  2. What theories or concepts taught in the class can be used to better understand the problem? Why?    
  3. Use the theories or concepts taught in the class to analyze at least one illustrative example.

Language: English 

Length: 6-minute presentation + 3-minute Q&A 

Presentation schedule: To be announced on Blackboard during Week 4

Assessment criteria:

  • Demonstrate understanding of theories or concepts discussed in the class and be able to use them to explain a real-world and language-based problem in social life
  • Clear, coherent expression, engaging presentation style
  • Effective use of visuals/multimedia/Powerpoint
  • Check the rubric on Blackboard for more details

STATEMENT ON GENERATIVE AI & MT

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Upload your PowerPoint slides to Blackboard (CHIN2600 Blackboard > Assessment > In-Class Presentation in English > PowerPoint Slides Submission) the day before your presentation.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
CHIN2600 Final Chinese Video

Purpose: Synthesize your learning, both theoretical and practical, and propose solutions to the identified problem.  

Due Date: November 9 at 15:00 pm (Brisbane time)

Weight: 40%.

Video length: within 10 minutes  

Description: Create a video analyzing Chinese social interactional data collected from your own social life, and relate your findings to a concept and theory discussed in class. You will also collect English data on a similar phenomenon to compare and contrast with your Chinese data. Use the findings from your analysis to propose solutions to the problem you have identified. You are encouraged to base this video on the problem you identified in your in-class presentation project, though you are welcome to focus on a different topic if you prefer.

What to include:

The video should have the following components: 

  1. An introduction that introduces a real-world and language-based problem and the importance of the problem;
  2. A theoretical section that briefly explains what theories you are using in analysing the data and why they are the best theories for analysing the data. The theories must be from the readings covered in class; 
  3. A data section that briefly explains how the Chinese and English data were collected and selected; 
  4. A data analysis section that presents your analysis with at least four illustrative (2 Chinese and 2 English) examples. This is the main part of the video; 
  5. A solution section that uses one of the solution approaches taught in class and has at least three suggestions for the identified problem. The suggestions should be based on the findings of the data analysis. This is also the main part of the video;  
  6. Conclusion section that summarizes your findings; 
  7. A reference list in the end that includes sources you referenced in the paper. In the reference list, provide the URL to your interview/interactional recordings if you use them as your data. 
  • The powerpoint slides should follow APA https://owl.purdue.edu/owl/research_and_citation/apa_style/apa_formatting_and_style_guide/general_format.html
  • Make sure to relate the analysis to the themes, theoretical frameworks, or concepts studied in class. 
  • Your data analysis must include transcripts (interactional data) or texts (screenshots). Transcripts should have Chinese characters (Chinese data) and English words (English data).
  • Upload your slides and video to Blackboard
  • The video needs to show your face and student ID without adding any filters. 

Submission: Upload your video to Blackboard (CHIN2600 Blackboard > Assessment > Final Chinese Video and Interview > Final video submission). You have unlimited attempts to submit before the due date and time.

Assessment criteria: Check Guidelines provided on Blackboard

STATEMENT ON GENERATIVE AI & MT

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
